nodes.title: Reset pending timer before scheduling
nodes.why: When events come in quickly (like typing), we only want the latest one to win. Clearing the old timer prevents stale calls from running.
nodes.alternative: Start a timer only once and ignore the rest until it fires (that behaves more like throttle).
nodes.tradeoff: This gives true trailing debounce behavior, but if input never pauses, execution keeps getting pushed back.

nodes.title: Schedule latest call with current context
nodes.why: After the user stops, we run exactly one call using the latest arguments and the correct `this` context.
nodes.alternative: Store `lastArgs` and `lastThis` explicitly, then invoke them in the timeout callback.
nodes.tradeoff: Inline `apply` is compact and easy to read, while explicit state is easier to extend for richer APIs.

nodes.title: Keep timer state in closure
nodes.why: The timer id has to survive between calls. A closure is the simplest way to keep that shared state.
nodes.alternative: Store timers in an external map keyed by function or component id.
nodes.tradeoff: Closure state keeps the implementation small and local, but external stores can be easier to inspect globally.

nodes.title: Track latest args/context for delayed execution
nodes.why: `flush()` and the delayed callback should run with the most recent input, not an older one from earlier in the burst.
nodes.alternative: Run immediately with current args and avoid storing any "last" state.
nodes.tradeoff: Remembering last args/context unlocks better control APIs, but it does add more mutable state to manage.

nodes.title: Expose cancel to drop pending work
nodes.why: In real apps, users navigate away or components unmount. `cancel()` gives a safe way to stop pending work.
nodes.alternative: Return only a plain debounced function and force callers to manage cancellation themselves.
nodes.tradeoff: Built-in cancel is ergonomic and safer, but the returned value is no longer a plain function.

nodes.title: Expose flush for immediate execution
nodes.why: `flush()` is useful when you want pending work to run right now (for example, right before submit).
nodes.alternative: Wait for the timer to finish naturally, or call the original function outside debounce.
nodes.tradeoff: It gives precise control to callers, but adds one more behavior they need to understand.

nodes.title: Gate immediate execution for leading mode
nodes.why: In leading mode, we want the first call to run immediately, then stay quiet until the cooldown ends. This guard enforces that.
nodes.alternative: Use timestamp math to decide whether enough time has passed since the last run.
nodes.tradeoff: Timer-based gating is easier to read; timestamp logic can be more flexible for edge timing rules.

nodes.title: Reset debounce window on every call
nodes.why: Every new event extends the quiet window. Clearing and rescheduling ensures we react to the latest user intent.
nodes.alternative: Keep the original timer and ignore newer events until it fires.
nodes.tradeoff: Better UX for typing/search, but nonstop activity can postpone execution for a long time.

nodes.title: Invoke trailing call only when not in leading mode
nodes.why: If we already fired on the leading edge, firing again on the trailing edge would duplicate work for the same burst.
nodes.alternative: Expose a `trailing` flag and allow both leading and trailing calls when desired.
nodes.tradeoff: This keeps behavior simple and predictable, but it is less configurable than a full leading+trailing API.
